title: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS

items.title: Neurocysticercosis: Mechanism of Seizures and Diagnostic Imaging
items.content: Neurocysticercosis, caused by Taenia solium, is a leading parasitic CNS infection. It often presents with new-onset partial seizures due to cysts in brain parenchyma causing inflammation as they degenerate. MRI or CT scans are crucial for diagnosis, revealing different stages of the disease: vesicular (cystic lesions), colloidal (peripheral enhancement and edema), granulonodular (homogenous enhancement), and nodular-calcified (calcifications).
keyPoints: Neurocysticercosis is the most common parasitic CNS disease worldwide.
Seizures are a primary clinical manifestation, often due to inflammatory response as cysts degenerate.
MRI or CT scans visualize stages: vesicular (no edema), colloidal (peripheral enhancement and edema), granulonodular (homogenous enhancement, no edema), nodular-calcified (calcifications).
Susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) is sensitive for detecting small calcific foci.
Enzyme-linked immunotransfer blot recommended for confirmatory diagnosis.
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · ETIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Toxoplasmosis: Clinical Presentation and Diagnostic Approach in Immunocompromised Patients
items.content: Toxoplasmosis, caused by T. gondii, often remains asymptomatic but can reactivate in immunocompromised hosts, leading to CNS symptoms like headache, fever, seizures, and focal deficits. MRI findings include multiple lesions with ringed or nodular enhancement surrounded by edema in deep white matter, thalamus, basal ganglia, and gray-white junctions. Serum IgG antibody testing for T. gondii is essential when characteristic imaging abnormalities are present.
keyPoints: Primary Toxoplasma infection often asymptomatic; reactivation occurs mainly in immunocompromised hosts.
CNS symptoms include headache, fever, seizures, and focal deficits during reactivation.
MRI shows multiple lesions with ringed or nodular enhancement and surrounding edema.
Serum IgG antibody to T. gondii should be obtained when imaging suggests infection.
Positive serum test warrants treatment for toxoplasmosis.
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · ETIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Management of Neurocysticercosis with Anticysticidal Therapy
items.content: Anticysticidal therapy is indicated for neurocysticercosis in patients presenting with viable cysts, specifically those in the vesicular or colloidal stages. Albendazole monotherapy is recommended for one to two parenchymal cysts, while a combination of albendazole and praziquantel is advised for more than two viable cysts. Prednisone or dexamethasone should be started before anticysticidal therapy to mitigate the inflammatory response.
keyPoints: Albendazole dose: 15 mg/kg per day in two doses for 10–14 days.
Praziquantel dose: 50 mg/kg per day for 10–14 days.
Viable cysts are those in vesicular or colloidal stages.
Prednisone or dexamethasone is used to reduce inflammation before anticysticidal therapy.
Calcified lesions do not require anticysticidal treatment.
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · Infectious Focal CNS Lesions

items.title: Treatment Protocol for CNS Toxoplasmosis
items.content: CNS toxoplasmosis is treated with a combination of sulfadiazine, pyrimethamine, and folinic acid. This regimen continues until no active disease is evident on neuroimaging, typically requiring at least 6 weeks. Clindamycin plus pyrimethamine serves as an alternative for those intolerant to sulfadiazine.
keyPoints: Sulfadiazine dose: 1.5–2.0 g orally qid initially, reduced to 2–4 g/d after 6 weeks.
Pyrimethamine loading dose: 100 mg orally, then 75–100 mg daily, reduced to 50 mg/d after 6 weeks.
Folinic acid dose: 10–15 mg orally daily to prevent megaloblastic anemia.
Therapy duration: At least 6 weeks until no active disease on imaging.
Clindamycin plus pyrimethamine is an alternative for sulfadiazine intolerance.
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · Infectious Focal CNS Lesions

items.title: Epidemiology of Subdural Empyema and Its Predisposing Conditions
items.content: Subdural empyema (SDE) is a rare but serious condition, accounting for 15–25% of focal suppurative CNS infections. It predominantly affects young males with an approximate 3:1 male-to-female ratio, largely due to its association with sinusitis, particularly frontal sinusitis. The incidence of SDE as a complication of severe frontal sinusitis requiring hospitalization is about 1-2%. Other predisposing factors include head trauma and neurosurgery.
keyPoints: SDE accounts for 15–25% of focal suppurative CNS infections
Most common predisposing condition: Sinusitis, especially frontal sinuses
Male predominance with ~3:1 male/female ratio
70% of cases occur in the second and third decades of life
SDE may complicate 1–2% of severe hospitalization-requiring frontal sinusitis
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · SUBDURAL EMPYEMA | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Pathophysiology and Complications Associated with Subdural Empyema
items.content: Subdural empyema often arises from sinusitis-associated infections, spreading via retrograde infection of mucosal veins or contiguous spread from osteomyelitis. It can also occur due to direct bacterial introduction during neurosurgery. The subdural space allows rapid infection spread, typically starting in the upper and anterior cerebral hemisphere portions. SDE frequently coexists with other intracranial infections such as epidural empyema (40%), cortical thrombophlebitis (35%), and intracranial abscess or cerebritis (>25%). Cortical venous infarction can lead to necrosis, focal neurological deficits, and seizures.
keyPoints: SDE develops from retrograde spread of infection or contiguous spread
Rapid evolution due to large subdural space with few barriers
Commonly starts in the upper and anterior portions of a cerebral hemisphere
Associated intracranial infections: Epidural empyema (40%), cortical thrombophlebitis (35%), intracranial abscess/cerebritis (>25%)
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · SUBDURAL EMPYEMA | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ETIOLOGY | ■ PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Recognizing Symptoms and Risks in Subdural Empyema (SDE)
items.content: Patients with SDE often present with fever, worsening headache, and new CNS symptoms. In those with known sinusitis, the development of focal neurologic deficits, seizures, nuchal rigidity, or increased intracranial pressure (ICP) should raise suspicion for SDE. Headaches typically start localized but become generalized as the condition progresses.
keyPoints: Fever and progressively worsening headache are common initial symptoms.
New CNS signs/symptoms in patients with known sinusitis suggest possible SDE.
Common focal neurologic deficits include contralateral hemiparesis or hemiplegia.
Seizures often begin as partial motor seizures, becoming generalized later.
Progressive deterioration in consciousness can lead to coma if untreated.
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · CLINICAL PRESENTATION | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnosing SDE: Imaging and CSF Considerations
items.content: MRI, especially with gadolinium enhancement, is superior for diagnosing SDE compared to CT. It helps delineate the empyema from brain parenchyma and identify associated conditions like sinusitis or cortical venous infarction. CT may show disproportionate mass effect relative to the size of the SDE. CSF examination should be avoided due to lack of diagnostic benefit and risk of cerebral herniation.
keyPoints: MRI with gadolinium is preferred for diagnosing SDE, enhancing empyema delineation.
CT can reveal a crescent-shaped hypodense lesion with disproportionate mass effect.
CSF examination should be avoided in suspected SDE due to risk of herniation.
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · CLINICAL PRESENTATION | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Key Features Differentiating SDE from Other CNS Infections
items.content: Subdural empyema (SDE) presents with headache, fever, focal neurologic signs, and rapid progression to altered consciousness. Unlike bacterial meningitis, which also features nuchal rigidity, SDE is associated with significant focal deficits. Nuchal rigidity in the presence of these symptoms suggests SDE over brain abscess or epidural empyema.
keyPoints: Nuchal rigidity unusual with brain abscess or epidural empyema
Significant focal neurologic signs and fever suggest SDE
Bacterial meningitis presents nuchal rigidity but typically lacks severe focal deficits
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Subdural Empyema | PROGNOSIS

items.title: Management and Prognosis of SDE
items.content: SDE is a medical emergency requiring emergent neurosurgical intervention, such as craniotomy or burr-hole drainage. Initial empirical antibiotic therapy includes third-generation cephalosporins, vancomycin, and metronidazole for community-acquired cases. Hospital-acquired SDE may require carbapenems due to Pseudomonas spp. or MRSA. Therapy duration is typically 3–4 weeks post-drainage, extended if cranial osteomyelitis is present. Prognosis depends on initial consciousness level, empyema size, and therapy speed, with up to 50% experiencing long-term neurologic sequelae.
keyPoints: Emergent neurosurgical evacuation required
Empirical antibiotics: third-generation cephalosporin, vancomycin, metronidazole
Hospital-acquired SDE may need carbapenems and vancomycin
Antibiotic therapy duration: 3–4 weeks post-drainage
Prognosis affected by initial consciousness level, empyema size, therapy speed
Up to 50% experience long-term neurologic sequelae
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | Subdural Empyema | PROGNOSIS

items.title: Understanding Cranial Epidural Abscess Etiology and Pathophysiology
items.content: Cranial epidural abscesses are less common than brain abscesses or subdural empyemas (SDE), accounting for <2% of focal suppurative CNS infections. They typically develop as complications from craniotomies, compound skull fractures, or through the spread of infection from adjacent structures like the frontal sinuses, middle ear, mastoid, or orbit. Unlike SDEs, epidural abscesses require dissection of the dura away from the inner skull table to expand, often resulting in smaller lesions.
keyPoints: Cranial epidural abscess accounts for <2% of focal suppurative CNS infections
Develops as a complication of craniotomy or compound skull fracture, or through spread from frontal sinuses, middle ear, mastoid, or orbit
Infection must dissect the dura away from the inner skull table to expand
Epidural abscesses are often smaller than SDEs due to anatomical constraints
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Recognizing Clinical Presentation of Cranial Epidural Abscess
items.content: Patients with cranial epidural abscess typically present with fever (60%), headache (40%), nuchal rigidity (35%), seizures (10%), and focal deficits (5%). Symptoms may develop insidiously due to slow enlargement in the confined space between the dura and inner skull table. Periorbital edema and Pott's puffy tumor, indicative of frontal bone osteomyelitis, are seen in ~40% of cases. In post-neurosurgical patients, wound infection is common, with altered mental status (45%), fever (35%), and headache (20%) as other possible symptoms.
keyPoints: Fever present in 60%, headache in 40%, nuchal rigidity in 35%
Seizures occur in 10% and focal deficits in 5%
Periorbital edema and Pott's puffy tumor seen in ~40%
In post-neurosurgical patients, altered mental status (45%), fever (35%), headache (20%)
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · ETIOLOGY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY | CLINICAL PRESENTATION

items.title: Optimal Imaging for Cranial Epidural Abscess Diagnosis
items.content: Cranial MRI with gadolinium enhancement is the preferred imaging modality to diagnose a cranial epidural abscess due to its superior sensitivity compared to CT, which can be limited by signal artifacts from skull bone.
keyPoints: MRI with gadolinium is the procedure of choice for demonstrating a cranial epidural abscess.
CT has limited sensitivity due to signal artifacts from the inner skull table.
On MRI, an epidural empyema appears as a lentiform or crescent-shaped fluid collection hyperintense on T2-weighted images and may be isointense or hypointense compared to brain on T1-weighted images.
Following gadolinium administration, there is linear enhancement of the dura on T1-weighted images.
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · DIAGNOSIS | Epidural Abscess | PROGNOSIS | DEFINITION | ANATOMY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY

items.title: Prognosis and Mortality Rate for Epidural Abscess
items.content: With modern medical interventions, the mortality rate for epidural abscess is less than 5%, and most survivors experience full recovery.
keyPoints: Mortality rate is <5% in modern series.
Full recovery is common among survivors.
items.reference: NONBACTERIAL CAUSES OF INFECTIOUS FOCAL CNS LESIONS · DIAGNOSIS | Epidural Abscess | PROGNOSIS | DEFINITION | ANATOMY AND PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
